taskVersion.name: epu_q2_short
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_short")
def epu_q2_short(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, short_pre, short_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

Archi

TARGET ARTICLE START
There is war outside the Miami Arena, on the east side, near the big steps. The gladiators are parking attendants, armed with orange flags. Battles occur on game nights only. Go tonight, to the Heat-Raptors game, for another exhibit by the parking lot mercenaries: * Oscar Freixas, 32, who runs a cheap lot ($3) about two blocks from the turnstiles, hoists a portable basketball goal atop a chain-link fence. ``Pay $2 if you make HOOP!!'' says his sign. * Lazaro Montero, 57, whose $7 spaces are one block closer, utilizes the scream-at-all-approaching-cars method. ``Otoyyyy!'' he belts out in street opera. * Jeffrey Stanley, 33, works a $10 lot in the high-rent district directly across from the Arena. He does it all: Vicious flag waving. Body language. High-decibel persuasion. ``Yo, yo, yo! Those lots are full,'' he bellows at a passing car, badmouthing his competitors. He is lying. But that's OK. Lying is tolerated in the parking lot wars. Also permitted: shifting traffic cones, dashing between cars and luring late arrivals with the vague promise of a discount. ``Can I get in for five bucks?'' asked one fan who recently rolled up to Stanley's lot. ``See the guy in the white shirt'' inside the lot, Stanley said, his head moving in something between a nod and a circle. ``He's got a gtod deal for you.'' At most big-city arenas, the controlled spots go for a single price. But in downtown Miami, it has always been a distance-for-dollars approach. The closer you park, the shorter the w

[...TRUNCATED...]

xt. Miamins skyline, its office buildings rising high and bright to the south, seem close enough to touch. As the Mercedes-Benzes, Hondas and Lexuses inch toward the arena, their electric windows open in a slow fall. The attendants approach, going to work, each loyal to their own philosophy of luring them past the gate. For Freixas, who sets up shop on Ninth Street, just west of the Metromover tracks, success comes with gimmicks. For Heat games, he asks his lone attendant, Bennie Chavis, 45, to hoist the basketball htop and pump up a ball. For Panthers games, they give away free rubber rats. ``They got me holding a big rat out here,'' Chavis says. ``It's embarrassing. But it works.'' Montero, meanwhile, stands in the street a few blocks west. He speaks little English, relying on volume and a flag-waving style that would humble an aircraft carrier signalman. Stanley, who wears an orange safety vest as he roams widely on Eighth Street, is convinced that no customer is lost as long as he has his ear. Tiptoeing into traffic, he has a big back of tricks -- the respectful compliment (``How ya doing, Doc?''), the rush job (``Don't be late to the game!''), and the direct confrontation (``Where are you gonna park?!''). ``You have to be very aggressive'' he says. ``You only have about a half a second to get their attention.'' Cutline JON KRAL / Herald Staff Caption: photo: PARK HERE: Cesar Obando waves cars into a $5 parking lot on NE Seventh Street. (a)
TARGET ARTICLE END

Archiv
